meta.title: W463 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W463 engine — OM602, OM603, OM642, M113, M157, M279. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM642 oil‑cooler seal risk explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes W463 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W463 engine typically costs £1,500–£18,000 supply only [MB‑QUOTE], depending on engine — the early four‑cylinder units run £1,500–£3,000, while the M279 V12 in the G 65 AMG can cost £10,000–£18,000. Fitting adds £780–£1,560. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W463 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM603 3.0‑litre six‑cylinder diesel (300 GD) is legendary for its durability — it's a simple, cast‑iron block unit that can exceed 300,000 miles with regular maintenance. Among petrols, the M113 V8 is robust, while the OM642 V6 diesel is reliable but suffers from oil‑cooler seal leaks.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes W463?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM642 V6 diesels suffer oil‑cooler seal leaks; M113/M273 V8 petrols can develop timing‑chain stretch; M157 V8s (G 63) can experience cam lifter wear; M279 V12s (G 65) have expensive coil pack and turbo issues; and early diesels can suffer from injection pump wear. Regular servicing is critical across all engines.

It depends heavily on the engine and era. Early diesels (OM602/OM603) are near‑bulletproof. The OM642 V6 diesel is robust but has a well‑documented oil‑cooler seal weakness. The M113 V8 is solid, while the M157 and M279 are high‑performance engines that demand meticulous maintenance. Overall, a well‑maintained W463 can be exceptionally durable.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W463 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright "avoid" — but a high‑mileage G 65 AMG (M279 V12) without full service history should be approached with extreme caution due to eye‑wateringly expensive parts. Similarly, an early G 55 AMG (M113 supercharged) with neglected supercharger maintenance warrants careful inspection.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W463 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs include: cold‑start rattle (M113/M273 chain), burning oil smell (OM642 seals), a ticking from the top end (M157 lifters), rough idle or hesitation (injection pump on early diesels), or misfires on V12s (coil packs). If you experience any of these, get a diagnosis immediately — early intervention saves thousands.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes W463 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £40,000 W463, a £5,500 OM642 recon is 14% of the car's value — safe if the car is otherwise sound. Against a tired £12,000 example, the same recon is 46% — safe but approaching our 70% Mercedes threshold. Use our 70% rule as your guide.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W463 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance, the OM603 diesel can exceed 300,000 miles, and the OM602 diesel is similarly durable. The M113 V8 can reach 200,000+ miles with regular oil changes. The OM642 V6 diesel is robust but the oil‑cooler seals will need replacement at around 100,000 miles. The M157 and M279 are durable but demand expensive preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W463 and W460?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W460 was the first‑generation G‑Class (1979–1991) with part‑time four‑wheel drive and leaf‑spring rear suspension. The W463 (1990–2018, this page) introduced permanent 4MATIC all‑wheel drive, ABS, and a more refined interior. The W463 also offered more powerful engine options, including AMG V8s and the V12.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W463 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and in the vehicle documentation. The code is stamped on the engine block — on early diesels it's on the left side, on OM642/M113 it's on the front left, and on M157/M279 it's on the right side. Tell us your variant and registration year, and we'll identify it for you.

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes G‑Class W463 Engine Codes — The Complete Database

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: OM642 ratings: 95% confidence, inherited from the Model Page where one exists. All other ratings: Expert‑knowledge tier, derived from cross‑referencing specialist and owner sources — confirm against the Model Page before publishing.

overview.intro: The W463 is the second generation of the Mercedes‑Benz G‑Class, produced from 1990 to 2018. It succeeded the W460 and introduced permanent 4MATIC all‑wheel drive, anti‑lock brakes, and a trio of electronically‑locking differentials — making it one of the most capable off‑road vehicles ever built. A major facelift in 2012 brought new engines — including the 5.5‑litre twin‑turbo V8 in the G 63 AMG and the 6.0‑litre V12 in the G 65 AMG — and updated interior and exterior styling. The W463 was available as a closed off‑roader and, from 1990 to 2011, as a cabriolet. Its engine range spans from the 2.3‑litre four‑cylinder petrol to the 6.0‑litre twin‑turbo V12.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz W463 • Predecessor: W460 • Successor: W464 • Sibling body styles in this generation: Cabriolet (1990–2011).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: No W463‑specific enquiry figure is available; OM642 diesel enquiries dominate the broader Mercedes compact‑car parc, with 880 UK requests in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid V6 — reliable, but chain watch
V8 Grunt — robust, but chain watch
AMG Classic — high performance, high cost
V6 Torque — smooth, but seals leak
AMG Tax — high performance, high cost
AMG Modern — high performance, high cost

economicsRule.title: The W463 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £15,000–£60,000 W463 sits at 5–37% of the car’s value — generally safe. Against a sub‑£15,000 example, the same recon can brush our 70% Mercedes threshold — we’ll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £40,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 8–14% — a straightforward repair decision. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W463 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM602/OM603 diesels — simple, robust, cheap to fix.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W463 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M279 V12 — AMG V12 parts and labour are eye‑wateringly expensive.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W463 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: Post‑2012 G 350 BlueTEC (OM642) — modern diesel capability without V8 running costs.

problems.title: OM642 Oil‑Cooler Seal Leak
problems.affectedModels: W463 G 320 CDI, G 350 CDI, G 350 BlueTEC (OM642 V6 diesel).
problems.rootCause: The oil‑cooler O‑rings in the V of the OM642 engine degrade with heat cycling, causing oil leaks that can drip onto the exhaust and create a burning smell. Labour is intensive as the intake manifold must be removed.
problems.recommendation: If you notice a burning oil smell or oil residue in the engine V, have the oil‑cooler seals replaced immediately — an £800–£1,500 repair is cheaper than the £3,200+ replacement that follows if the engine runs low on oil.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W463 →

problems.title: M113 / M273 Timing‑Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The timing chain on M113 and M273 V8 engines can stretch over time, particularly if oil changes have been neglected. Rattle on start‑up is the classic warning sign.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rattle on start‑up, get the chain inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £3,500+ replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M113 / M273 engine prices for your W463 →

problems.title: M157 Cam Lifter & Turbo Wear (G 63 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The high‑output M157 engine can suffer from cam lifter wear and turbocharger issues, particularly if oil changes have been neglected. Ticking from the top end is the classic warning sign.
problems.recommendation: If you’re buying a G 63 AMG, budget for a cam lifter inspection and insist on full service history — a £1,500–£4,000 preventative repair is a fraction of the £7,000+ replacement that follows a full failure. This is the price of AMG ownership.

cta.label: Compare M157 engine prices for your G 63 AMG →

problems.title: M279 Coil Pack & Turbo Wear (G 65 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The V12 M279 engine has 12 individual coil packs that can fail, and the twin turbos can develop wear. Parts are extremely expensive and often specialist‑only.
problems.recommendation: If you’re buying a G 65 AMG, budget for a full inspection and insist on meticulous service history — coil pack replacement and turbo work are inevitable on high‑mileage examples. This is the ultimate AMG tax.

cta.label: Compare M279 engine prices for your G 65 AMG →

replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page’s baseline figures — W463 shares its engine bay architecture across its long production run, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. OM642 and M276 costs carried forward from the EM data’s Table A.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W463 closed off‑roader. The Cabriolet (1990–2011) shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
